prompt_md: A non-`const` global variable defined at file scope (no `static` keyword) has which linkage by default?

options.a: Internal linkage — it is private to the translation unit.
options.b: External linkage — it can be accessed from other translation units.
options.c: No linkage — it cannot be named outside its block.
options.d: Static linkage — it persists for the lifetime of the program.

explanation_md: Non-`const` global variables have external linkage by default in C++. Adding `static` at file scope changes this to internal linkage. `const` and `constexpr` globals are the exception — they default to internal linkage.

prompt_md: Which of the following gives a variable internal linkage at file scope?
explanation_md: `static` at file scope changes the linkage of a variable or function from external (the default for non-const globals) to internal, hiding it from other translation units. `extern` does the opposite — it declares a name with external linkage. Placement inside a function gives the name *no* linkage (it is a local variable).

`count` starts at `0` and `bump()` increments it each call. After three calls, `count` is `3`. The `static` keyword gives `count` and `bump` internal linkage, but since this is the only file, that has no observable runtime effect here.

prompt_md: You define `static int helper { 5 };` in a header file and include that header in three `.cpp` files. How many `helper` variables exist at runtime?

options.a: One shared variable across all three files.
options.b: Three independent variables, one per translation unit.
options.c: Zero — `static` in a header prevents the variable from being created.
options.d: The program fails to link because of duplicate definitions.

explanation_md: Each `.cpp` file that includes the header gets its own translation unit, and `static` gives each definition internal linkage. So there are three separate variables named `helper`, one per translation unit — not one shared variable. This is almost never what the programmer intends when placing `static` definitions in headers.
